expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of covalent bonding, examining how atoms share electrons to achieve stable electron configurations, forming molecular compounds with distinctive properties including lower melting points, directional bonding, and diverse molecular geometries.

core_concept.principle: Covalent bonds form when atoms share pairs of valence electrons to achieve stable noble gas configurations, creating molecular compounds held together by shared electron attraction between nuclei.
core_concept.explanation: Covalent bonding occurs when atoms with similar electronegativities interact, typically between nonmetals. Instead of transferring electrons (as in ionic bonding), atoms share electron pairs so each atom can count the shared electrons toward completing its valence shell octet. The shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei simultaneously, creating a strong bond that holds the atoms together. The number of bonds an atom forms depends on how many electrons it needs to complete its octet. Covalent compounds form discrete molecules rather than crystal lattices, resulting in different physical properties than ionic compounds.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron sharing between two hydrogen atoms forming H₂ molecule
image.caption: Covalent bonding: atoms share electron pairs so each can achieve stable electron configuration.

sections.section_title: The Covalent Bonding Process: Electron Sharing
sections.introduction: Covalent bonding occurs when atoms share valence electrons rather than transferring them. This sharing allows both atoms to achieve stable electron configurations while remaining electrically neutral, forming discrete molecules with unique properties.

key_concept.definition: A covalent bond is a chemical bond formed by the sharing of one or more pairs of valence electrons between atoms, typically between nonmetals with similar electronegativities.
key_concept.context: Covalent bonding typically occurs when electronegativity difference is less than 1.7, most commonly between nonmetal atoms in Groups 14-17.

principles.statement: Atoms share valence electrons so each atom can count the shared electrons toward completing its octet; shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei simultaneously.
principles.application: Predicts number of bonds: atoms share enough electron pairs to complete octets (C forms 4 bonds, N forms 3, O forms 2, F forms 1).
principles.example: Two H atoms (1 valence e⁻ each) share their electrons: H:H or H-H. Each H counts 2 electrons (duet rule satisfied).
principles.analogy: Like sharing an umbrella: both people (atoms) benefit from the shared resource (electrons) without giving up ownership.

image.alt: Two hydrogen atoms sharing electrons to form H₂ molecule
image.caption: Electron sharing: each H contributes 1 electron; both share the pair, achieving duet configuration.

principles.statement: Covalent bond formation releases energy (exothermic); bond energy is the energy required to break the bond or released when it forms.
principles.application: Explains why molecules are more stable than separated atoms; stronger bonds (higher bond energy) create more stable molecules.
principles.example: H-H bond energy = 436 kJ/mol (energy released when bond forms). O=O bond energy = 498 kJ/mol. N≡N bond energy = 945 kJ/mol (very strong).
principles.analogy: Like a handshake: forming the connection (bond) is natural and stable; breaking it requires effort (energy input).

image.alt: Energy diagram showing energy release when covalent bond forms
image.caption: Bond formation releases energy: bonded atoms have lower potential energy than separated atoms.

principles.statement: Covalent bonds form when atomic orbitals overlap, allowing shared electrons to occupy the space between nuclei where they're attracted to both.
principles.application: Explains bond directionality and strength: greater orbital overlap = stronger bond; explains molecular geometry.
principles.example: H₂ formation: two 1s orbitals overlap, creating a bonding molecular orbital between nuclei where electron density is highest.
principles.analogy: Like two flashlights pointing at the same spot: the overlapping light (electron density) is brightest between the sources (nuclei).

image.alt: Diagram showing atomic orbital overlap forming covalent bond
image.caption: Orbital overlap: atomic orbitals merge to create bonding region with high electron density between nuclei.

components.definition: Covalent bond strength depends on orbital overlap, bond length, and number of shared electron pairs.
components.interpretation: Greater orbital overlap and shorter bond length create stronger bonds; multiple bonds (double, triple) are stronger than single bonds.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Energy Change in Covalent Bond Formation
worked_examples.problem: When two hydrogen atoms form an H₂ molecule, 436 kJ/mol is released. If you form 0.5 mol of H₂ from H atoms, how much energy is released? Is this process exothermic or endothermic?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the bond energy per mole of H₂ formed.
step_by_step.calculation: H-H bond energy = 436 kJ/mol (released when 1 mol H₂ forms)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy for 0.5 mol of H₂.
step_by_step.calculation: 0.5 mol H₂ × 436 kJ/mol = 218 kJ released

step_by_step.explanation: Determine if exothermic or endothermic.
step_by_step.calculation: Energy released = exothermic (ΔH < 0)

worked_examples.final_answer: 218 kJ of energy is released when 0.5 mol of H₂ forms. This is an exothermic process (ΔH = -218 kJ) because energy is released when bonds form. This demonstrates why covalent bond formation is energetically favorable.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond energy calculations and thermodynamics of covalent bond formation

sections.section_title: Types of Covalent Bonds: Single, Double, and Triple
sections.introduction: Covalent bonds are classified by the number of electron pairs shared between atoms. Single bonds share one pair, double bonds share two pairs, and triple bonds share three pairs. Bond multiplicity affects bond strength, length, and molecular properties.

key_concept.definition: Covalent bonds are classified by the number of shared electron pairs: single bond (1 pair, 2 e⁻), double bond (2 pairs, 4 e⁻), or triple bond (3 pairs, 6 e⁻).
key_concept.context: Multiple bonds form when atoms need to share more electrons to complete octets; triple bonds are strongest and shortest, single bonds are weakest and longest.

classifications.value: One shared electron pair (2 electrons) between two atoms
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms share one electron pair; allows rotation around bond axis; most common bond type in organic molecules.

examples.explanation: Two H atoms share 1 electron pair: H-H. Bond length = 74 pm, bond energy = 436 kJ/mol. Each H achieves duet (2 e⁻).

image.alt: H₂ molecule showing single covalent bond
image.caption: Single bond: one shared electron pair between two atoms.

examples.explanation: CH₄ has four C-H single bonds. C shares 1 e⁻ with each of 4 H atoms. C achieves octet (8 e⁻); each H achieves duet (2 e⁻). Bond length = 109 pm, bond energy = 413 kJ/mol.
C₂H₆ has C-C single bond connecting two carbon atoms. Free rotation around C-C bond allows different conformations. Bond length = 154 pm, bond energy = 348 kJ/mol.

classifications.value: Two shared electron pairs (4 electrons) between two atoms
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms need to share 4 electrons to complete octets; creates rigid, planar structures; common in C=O, C=C, O=O bonds.

examples.explanation: Two O atoms share 2 electron pairs: O=O. Each O has 6 valence e⁻, needs 2 more. Bond length = 121 pm, bond energy = 498 kJ/mol. Paramagnetic due to unpaired electrons.

image.alt: O₂ molecule showing double covalent bond
image.caption: Double bond: two shared electron pairs create stronger, shorter bond than single.

examples.explanation: CO₂ has two C=O double bonds: O=C=O. C shares 2 e⁻ pairs with each O. Linear molecule, bond length = 116 pm, bond energy = 799 kJ/mol per C=O bond.
C₂H₄ has C=C double bond. No rotation around double bond creates cis/trans isomerism. Bond length = 134 pm (shorter than C-C 154 pm), bond energy = 614 kJ/mol.

classifications.value: Three shared electron pairs (6 electrons) between two atoms
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms need to share 6 electrons to complete octets; very strong and short; linear geometry around triple bond; rare but important.

examples.explanation: Two N atoms share 3 electron pairs: N≡N. Each N has 5 valence e⁻, needs 3 more. Bond length = 110 pm, bond energy = 945 kJ/mol (strongest covalent bond). Explains N₂ inertness.

image.alt: N₂ molecule showing triple covalent bond
image.caption: Triple bond: three shared electron pairs create strongest, shortest covalent bond.

examples.explanation: C₂H₂ (acetylene) has C≡C triple bond. Linear geometry (180° bond angle). Bond length = 120 pm, bond energy = 839 kJ/mol. Used in welding torches.

image.alt: Ethyne molecule showing C≡C triple bond
image.caption: C≡C triple bond: linear geometry, very strong bond used in acetylene torches.

examples.explanation: HCN has C≡N triple bond. Linear molecule. C shares 3 pairs with N, 1 pair with H. Important in organic chemistry and biochemistry.

principles.principle: Bond Length and Bond Energy Relationship
principles.statement: Bond length decreases and bond energy increases with bond multiplicity: triple < double < single (length); triple > double > single (energy).
principles.application: Predicts molecular stability and reactivity: shorter, stronger bonds are harder to break, making molecules less reactive.
principles.example: C-C: 154 pm, 348 kJ/mol; C=C: 134 pm, 614 kJ/mol; C≡C: 120 pm, 839 kJ/mol. Triple bond is 2.4× stronger than single.

image.alt: Graph comparing bond lengths and energies for single, double, triple bonds
image.caption: Bond multiplicity effect: more shared pairs = shorter, stronger bonds.

principles.analogy: Like ropes: one rope (single bond) is weaker and longer; three ropes bundled (triple bond) are stronger and shorter.

principles.statement: Single bonds allow free rotation around bond axis; double and triple bonds are rigid and prevent rotation, creating fixed geometries.
principles.application: Explains molecular conformations (single bonds) vs. geometric isomers (double bonds); affects molecular shape and properties.
principles.example: Ethane (C-C single): free rotation creates different conformations. Ethene (C=C double): rigid, creates cis/trans isomers with different properties.
principles.analogy: Like a door hinge: single bond = door swings freely; double bond = door is welded shut (fixed position).

image.alt: Diagram showing rotation around single vs. double bonds
image.caption: Bond rotation: single bonds rotate freely; double/triple bonds are rigid.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bond Strengths and Lengths
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following bonds in order of (a) increasing bond length and (b) increasing bond energy: C-C, C=C, C≡C. Explain the relationship.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify bond multiplicities.
step_by_step.calculation: C-C: single (1 pair); C=C: double (2 pairs); C≡C: triple (3 pairs)

step_by_step.explanation: Apply bond length trend (more pairs = shorter).
step_by_step.calculation: Bond length: C≡C (120 pm) < C=C (134 pm) < C-C (154 pm)

step_by_step.explanation: Apply bond energy trend (more pairs = stronger).
step_by_step.calculation: Bond energy: C-C (348 kJ/mol) < C=C (614 kJ/mol) < C≡C (839 kJ/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain the relationship.
step_by_step.calculation: More shared electron pairs = stronger attraction between nuclei = shorter, stronger bond

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Increasing bond length: C≡C < C=C < C-C (triple shortest, single longest). (b) Increasing bond energy: C-C < C=C < C≡C (single weakest, triple strongest). More shared electron pairs create stronger nuclear attraction, pulling atoms closer together and requiring more energy to break the bond.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond multiplicity effects on bond length and bond energy

sections.introduction: Lewis structures provide a visual representation of covalent bonding, showing how valence electrons are shared between atoms to complete octets. Mastering Lewis structure drawing is essential for understanding molecular structure and bonding.

key_concept.definition: A Lewis structure is a diagram showing valence electrons as dots and covalent bonds as lines between atoms, with all atoms (except H) achieving octets through shared and lone pair electrons.
key_concept.context: Lewis structures follow systematic rules: count valence electrons, arrange atoms, distribute electrons to satisfy octets, form multiple bonds if needed.

principles.statement: Systematic procedure: (1) Count total valence electrons, (2) Arrange atoms with least electronegative as center, (3) Draw single bonds, (4) Distribute remaining electrons as lone pairs, (5) Form multiple bonds if octets incomplete.
principles.application: Enables accurate Lewis structure drawing for any covalent molecule; predicts molecular geometry and bonding patterns.
principles.example: CO₂: (1) 4 + 2(6) = 16 e⁻; (2) C center; (3) O-C-O single bonds use 4 e⁻; (4) 12 e⁻ remain for O lone pairs; (5) C needs 8, form O=C=O double bonds.

image.alt: Step-by-step Lewis structure drawing for CO₂
image.caption: Lewis structure steps: count electrons, arrange atoms, distribute electrons, form multiple bonds if needed.

principles.analogy: Like building a house: foundation (count electrons), frame (arrange atoms), walls (single bonds), finishing (lone pairs), reinforcement (multiple bonds).

principles.statement: In Lewis structures, all atoms (except H) must have 8 electrons around them (counting shared and lone pairs); H needs only 2 electrons (duet rule).
principles.application: Validates Lewis structures: if any atom lacks octet, form multiple bonds; if atoms exceed octet (Period 3+), expanded octet is possible.
principles.example: H₂O: O has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs); each H has 2 e⁻ (1 bond). CO₂: C has 8 e⁻ (4 bonds); each O has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs).
principles.analogy: Like seating at a table: each atom needs 8 'seats' (electrons); bonds and lone pairs fill the seats.

image.alt: Lewis structures showing octet completion for various molecules
image.caption: Octet rule in Lewis structures: all atoms (except H) have 8 electrons around them.

principles.statement: Formal charge = valence electrons - (lone pair electrons + ½ bonding electrons); best Lewis structure has formal charges closest to zero.
principles.application: Determines most stable Lewis structure when multiple arrangements possible; identifies charge distribution in molecules and ions.
principles.example: CO₂: C formal charge = 4 - (0 + ½×8) = 0; O formal charge = 6 - (4 + ½×4) = 0. All formal charges = 0, confirming O=C=O is correct structure.
principles.analogy: Like balancing a budget: formal charge shows if an atom has its 'fair share' of electrons compared to neutral state.

image.alt: Formal charge calculation for CO₂ Lewis structure
image.caption: Formal charge: helps identify most stable Lewis structure when multiple arrangements possible.

components.definition: Lewis structure components include atomic symbols, bonding pairs (lines), lone pairs (dots), and formal charges.
components.interpretation: Formal charge = 0 is ideal; negative formal charge on more electronegative atoms is preferred; minimize formal charge magnitudes.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Water (H₂O)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for water (H₂O), showing all valence electrons, bonds, and lone pairs. Verify that all atoms satisfy the octet/duet rule.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: H: 2 atoms × 1 e⁻ = 2 e⁻; O: 1 atom × 6 e⁻ = 6 e⁻; Total = 8 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with O as center (H can only form 1 bond).
step_by_step.calculation: H-O-H arrangement

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between O and each H.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 4 e⁻ used; 8 - 4 = 4 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons as lone pairs on O.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 e⁻ remaining = 2 lone pairs on O; O now has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs); each H has 2 e⁻ (1 bond)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify octet/duet rule satisfaction.
step_by_step.calculation: O: 8 e⁻ ✓ (octet); H: 2 e⁻ each ✓ (duet). Structure complete.

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O Lewis structure: O center with 2 H atoms single-bonded, 2 lone pairs on O. Structure: H-Ö-H (with 2 lone pairs on O). All atoms satisfy octet/duet rule. Bent molecular geometry results from lone pair repulsion.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic Lewis structure drawing with octet/duet rule verification

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for CO₂. Show why double bonds are necessary to satisfy the octet rule for all atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 e⁻; O: 2 atoms × 6 e⁻ = 12 e⁻; Total = 16 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with C as center (least electronegative).
step_by_step.calculation: O-C-O arrangement

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds first.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 4 e⁻ used; 16 - 4 = 12 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons as lone pairs on O atoms.
step_by_step.calculation: 12 e⁻ = 6 lone pairs; 3 pairs per O; C has only 4 e⁻ (2 bonds) → incomplete octet!

step_by_step.explanation: Form double bonds to complete C octet.
step_by_step.calculation: Move 1 lone pair from each O to form C=O double bonds; C now has 8 e⁻ (4 bonds); each O has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify octet rule and calculate formal charges.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 8 e⁻ ✓; O: 8 e⁻ each ✓; Formal charges: C = 4-(0+4) = 0; O = 6-(4+2) = 0. All zero ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂ Lewis structure: O=C=O with 2 lone pairs on each O. Double bonds are necessary because single bonds leave C with only 4 electrons. Linear geometry (180° bond angle). All formal charges = 0, confirming this is the most stable structure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple bond formation to satisfy octet rule in Lewis structures

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Ammonium Ion (NH₄⁺)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for the ammonium ion (NH₄⁺). Account for the positive charge in your electron count and show the charge on the final structure.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons, accounting for charge.
step_by_step.calculation: N: 5 e⁻; H: 4 atoms × 1 e⁻ = 4 e⁻; Positive charge: -1 e⁻; Total = 5 + 4 - 1 = 8 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with N as center.
step_by_step.calculation: N center with 4 H atoms around it

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between N and each H.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 8 e⁻ used; 8 - 8 = 0 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Check octet rule and add charge notation.
step_by_step.calculation: N has 8 e⁻ (4 bonds) ✓; each H has 2 e⁻ (1 bond) ✓; Add brackets and + charge: [NH₄]⁺

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charge on N.
step_by_step.calculation: N formal charge = 5 - (0 + ½×8) = +1 ✓ (matches ion charge)

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₄⁺ Lewis structure: N center with 4 H atoms single-bonded, no lone pairs on N, enclosed in brackets with + charge: [NH₄]⁺. N has 8 e⁻ (octet); each H has 2 e⁻ (duet). Positive charge is on N (formal charge = +1). Tetrahedral geometry.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lewis structures for polyatomic ions with charge accounting

sections.section_title: Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Covalent Bonding
sections.introduction: While the octet rule applies to most main-group elements, important exceptions exist. Some atoms have incomplete octets (fewer than 8 electrons), while others (Period 3 and below) can have expanded octets (more than 8 electrons).

key_concept.definition: Octet rule exceptions include: incomplete octets (Be, B with <8 e⁻), expanded octets (Period 3+ elements with >8 e⁻ using d orbitals), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals with unpaired electrons).
key_concept.context: These exceptions occur due to atomic size, available orbitals, and electron count; understanding them is essential for accurate Lewis structure drawing.

classifications.value: Atoms with fewer than 8 valence electrons in stable molecules
characteristics: Common for Be (4 e⁻), B (6 e⁻)
classifications.behavior: Be and B don't have enough valence electrons to complete octet without forming charged species; accept electron pairs from other molecules.

examples.explanation: Be has 2 valence e⁻, forms 2 bonds with H. Be has only 4 e⁻ around it (incomplete octet). Linear molecule. Be is electron-deficient, acts as Lewis acid.

image.alt: BeH₂ Lewis structure showing incomplete octet on Be
image.caption: Incomplete octet: Be has only 4 valence electrons in BeH₂.

examples.explanation: B has 3 valence e⁻, forms 3 bonds with F. B has only 6 e⁻ around it (incomplete octet). Trigonal planar. Reacts with NH₃ to complete octet: BF₃ + NH₃ → F₃B-NH₃.

image.alt: BF₃ Lewis structure showing incomplete octet on B
image.caption: Incomplete octet: B has only 6 valence electrons in BF₃, accepts electron pair from NH₃.

classifications.value: Atoms with more than 8 valence electrons (Period 3 and below)
characteristics: Elements with available d orbitals (P, S, Cl, etc.)
classifications.behavior: Period 3+ elements can use empty d orbitals to accommodate more than 8 electrons; enables compounds like SF₆, PCl₅, ClF₃.

examples.explanation: S has 6 valence e⁻, forms 6 bonds with F. S has 12 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). Octahedral geometry. S uses 3d orbitals to accommodate extra electrons.

image.alt: SF₆ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on S
image.caption: Expanded octet: S has 12 valence electrons in SF₆ using d orbitals.

examples.explanation: P has 5 valence e⁻, forms 5 bonds with Cl. P has 10 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). Trigonal bipyramidal geometry. Used in organic synthesis.

image.alt: PCl₅ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on P
image.caption: Expanded octet: P has 10 valence electrons in PCl₅.

examples.explanation: Cl has 7 valence e⁻, forms 3 bonds with F, has 2 lone pairs. Cl has 10 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). T-shaped geometry. Extremely reactive oxidizing agent.

image.alt: ClF₃ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on Cl
image.caption: Expanded octet: Cl has 10 valence electrons in ClF₃ with 2 lone pairs.

classifications.value: Molecules with unpaired electrons, cannot satisfy octet for all atoms
classifications.behavior: Cannot pair all electrons; one atom has 7 electrons instead of 8; highly reactive due to unpaired electron seeking to pair.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, O has 6 e⁻, total = 11 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it (incomplete). Paramagnetic (unpaired e⁻). Important signaling molecule in biology.

image.alt: NO Lewis structure showing unpaired electron
image.caption: Odd-electron molecule: NO has 11 valence electrons, one unpaired.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, 2 O have 12 e⁻, total = 17 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it. Brown gas, air pollutant, paramagnetic. Dimerizes to N₂O₄ to pair electrons.

image.alt: NO₂ Lewis structure showing unpaired electron on N
image.caption: Odd-electron molecule: NO₂ has 17 valence electrons, highly reactive.

examples.explanation: Cl has 7 e⁻, 2 O have 12 e⁻, total = 19 e⁻ (odd). Cl has expanded octet with unpaired electron. Used for water treatment and bleaching.

image.alt: ClO₂ Lewis structure showing unpaired electron
image.caption: Odd-electron molecule: ClO₂ has 19 valence electrons, used in water treatment.

principles.statement: Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F) cannot have expanded octets because they lack accessible d orbitals; maximum is 8 electrons in 2s and 2p orbitals.
principles.application: Explains why CF₄ exists but CF₆ doesn't; why OF₂ exists but OF₄ doesn't; limits bonding patterns for Period 2 elements.
principles.example: C max = 4 bonds (8 e⁻); N max = 4 bonds (8 e⁻ in NH₄⁺); O max = 2 bonds + 2 lone pairs (8 e⁻); F max = 1 bond + 3 lone pairs (8 e⁻).
principles.analogy: Like parking spaces: Period 2 has only 4 spaces (1 s + 3 p orbitals); Period 3+ has 9 spaces (1 s + 3 p + 5 d orbitals).

image.alt: Orbital diagram showing Period 2 vs. Period 3 available orbitals
image.caption: Period 2 limitation: no d orbitals available, maximum 8 electrons in valence shell.

principles.statement: Expanded octets are most stable when central atom is large (Period 3+) and bonded to small, electronegative atoms (F, O, Cl).
principles.application: Predicts which elements form expanded octet compounds: SF₆ stable, OF₆ impossible; PCl₅ stable, NCl₅ impossible.
principles.example: S (Period 3) forms SF₆ (12 e⁻); O (Period 2) cannot form OF₆. P (Period 3) forms PCl₅ (10 e⁻); N (Period 2) cannot form NCl₅.
principles.analogy: Like a host at a party: larger host (bigger atom) can accommodate more guests (electrons); small host (Period 2) has limited capacity.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure with Expanded Octet: SF₄
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for SF₄ (sulfur tetrafluoride). Show the expanded octet on sulfur and calculate the formal charge on each atom.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 e⁻; F: 4 atoms × 7 e⁻ = 28 e⁻; Total = 34 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with S as center.
step_by_step.calculation: S center with 4 F atoms around it

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between S and each F.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 8 e⁻ used; 34 - 8 = 26 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Complete octets on F atoms with lone pairs.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 F × 6 e⁻ (3 lone pairs each) = 24 e⁻; 26 - 24 = 2 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons on S as lone pair.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 e⁻ = 1 lone pair on S; S has 10 e⁻ total (4 bonds + 1 lone pair) = expanded octet

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 - (2 + ½×8) = 0; F: 7 - (6 + ½×2) = 0. All formal charges = 0 ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₄ Lewis structure: S center with 4 F atoms single-bonded, 1 lone pair on S. S has 10 valence electrons (expanded octet using 3d orbitals). Seesaw molecular geometry. All formal charges = 0, confirming stable structure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Expanded octet Lewis structure drawing with formal charge verification

sections.introduction: Covalent compounds exhibit distinctive physical and chemical properties that differ markedly from ionic compounds. These properties result from molecular structure, intermolecular forces, and the nature of covalent bonding.

key_concept.definition: Covalent compounds are characterized by lower melting and boiling points than ionic compounds, poor electrical conductivity, variable solubility depending on polarity, and formation of discrete molecules rather than crystal lattices.
key_concept.context: These properties contrast with ionic compounds and reflect the molecular nature of covalent bonding with weak intermolecular forces between molecules.

classifications.value: Covalent compounds generally have lower melting and boiling points than ionic compounds
characteristics: Molecular compounds vs. network covalent exceptions
classifications.behavior: Melting/boiling requires overcoming intermolecular forces (weak), not breaking covalent bonds (strong); network covalent (diamond, SiO₂) are exceptions with very high mp.

examples.explanation: H₂O melts at 0°C, boils at 100°C. These temperatures reflect hydrogen bonding between molecules, not O-H bond breaking. Compare to ionic NaCl (mp 801°C).

image.alt: Water phase changes showing intermolecular force breaking
image.caption: Covalent compound mp/bp: reflects intermolecular forces, not covalent bond breaking.

examples.explanation: CO₂ sublimes at -78°C (goes directly from solid to gas). Weak London dispersion forces between CO₂ molecules. Compare to ionic MgO (mp 2852°C).

image.alt: Dry ice (solid CO₂) subliming
image.caption: CO₂ sublimation: weak intermolecular forces allow solid→gas transition at low temperature.

examples.explanation: Diamond (C) has mp >3500°C. Each C covalently bonded to 4 others in 3D network. Must break covalent bonds to melt, not just intermolecular forces.

image.alt: Diamond crystal structure showing network covalent bonding
image.caption: Network covalent: diamond's 3D covalent network gives extremely high melting point.

classifications.value: Covalent compounds generally do not conduct electricity in any state
classifications.behavior: No charged particles free to move; electrons localized in bonds; exceptions have delocalized electrons (graphite) or ionize in water (acids).

examples.explanation: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ (sucrose) dissolved in water doesn't conduct. Molecules remain intact, no ions formed. Compare to NaCl solution which conducts (ions present).

image.alt: Conductivity test showing sugar solution doesn't conduct
image.caption: Covalent compound conductivity: molecular compounds don't conduct because no ions present.

examples.explanation: Graphite conducts electricity due to delocalized π electrons between carbon layers. Exception to covalent compound rule. Used in electrodes and pencils.

image.alt: Graphite structure showing delocalized electrons
image.caption: Graphite exception: delocalized electrons between layers enable electrical conductivity.

examples.explanation: HCl(g) doesn't conduct, but HCl(aq) conducts. HCl ionizes in water: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. Covalent molecule becomes ions in solution.

image.alt: HCl ionization in water producing ions
image.caption: Acid ionization: covalent HCl becomes ions in water, enabling conductivity.

classifications.value: Covalent compound solubility depends on polarity: polar dissolves in polar, nonpolar dissolves in nonpolar
characteristics: Nonpolar covalent: insoluble in water, soluble in organic solvents
classifications.behavior: Polar molecules interact with water via dipole-dipole or H-bonding; nonpolar molecules can't overcome water's H-bonding network.

examples.explanation: C₂H₅OH is polar with -OH group. Forms H-bonds with water. Miscible in all proportions. Used as solvent for both polar and nonpolar substances.

image.alt: Ethanol mixing with water showing hydrogen bonding
image.caption: Polar covalent solubility: ethanol's -OH group forms H-bonds with water, enabling mixing.

examples.explanation: Hydrocarbons (oil) are nonpolar. Can't form H-bonds with water. Separate into layers. Oil dissolves in nonpolar solvents like hexane instead.

image.alt: Oil and water separating into layers
image.caption: Nonpolar covalent insolubility: oil can't H-bond with water, separates into layers.

examples.explanation: I₂ (nonpolar) dissolves in CCl₄ (nonpolar). Both have London dispersion forces. Purple solution forms. I₂ doesn't dissolve in water (polar).

image.alt: Iodine dissolving in carbon tetrachloride
image.caption: Nonpolar solubility: I₂ dissolves in nonpolar CCl₄ but not in polar water.

classifications.value: Covalent compounds exist as gases, liquids, or soft solids at room temperature
classifications.behavior: Physical state depends on molecular size and intermolecular force strength; larger molecules have stronger London forces, higher mp/bp.

examples.explanation: CH₄ is small, nonpolar. Weak London forces. Gas at room temperature (bp -161°C). Main component of natural gas.

image.alt: Methane gas molecules
image.caption: Small covalent molecules: weak intermolecular forces = gaseous at room temperature.

examples.explanation: H₂O is small but polar with H-bonding. Strong intermolecular forces. Liquid at room temperature (mp 0°C, bp 100°C). Essential for life.

image.alt: Liquid water showing hydrogen bonding network
image.caption: Medium covalent molecules with H-bonding: liquid at room temperature.

examples.explanation: C₂₅H₅₂ is large hydrocarbon. Strong London forces from large surface area. Soft solid at room temperature (mp 50-70°C). Used in candles.

image.alt: Paraffin wax solid structure
image.caption: Large covalent molecules: strong London forces = solid at room temperature.

principles.statement: Covalent bond strength (intramolecular) is much greater than intermolecular forces; melting/boiling breaks intermolecular forces, not covalent bonds.
principles.application: Explains why covalent compounds have low mp/bp despite strong covalent bonds; why molecules remain intact during phase changes.
principles.example: H₂O: O-H bond energy = 463 kJ/mol; H-bond between molecules = 20 kJ/mol. Boiling breaks H-bonds, not O-H bonds.
principles.analogy: Like a book: intermolecular forces = books on shelf (easy to separate); covalent bonds = pages in book (hard to separate).

image.alt: Diagram comparing intermolecular forces to intramolecular bonds
image.caption: Intermolecular vs. intramolecular: phase changes break intermolecular forces, not covalent bonds.

principles.statement: Larger covalent molecules have higher melting/boiling points due to stronger London dispersion forces from increased surface area and electron count.
principles.application: Predicts physical state from molecular size: small = gas, medium = liquid, large = solid; explains homologous series trends.
principles.example: Alkanes: CH₄ (gas, -161°C bp) < C₄H₁₀ (gas, -0.5°C) < C₈H₁₈ (liquid, 125°C) < C₂₀H₄₂ (solid, 343°C).
principles.analogy: Like Velcro: more hooks and loops (larger molecule) = stronger attachment (higher mp/bp).

image.alt: Graph showing boiling point increase with molecular size in alkane series
image.caption: Molecular size effect: larger molecules have stronger London forces, higher boiling points.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Physical Properties from Bonding Type
worked_examples.problem: Predict whether the following compounds are likely to be solids with high melting points or gases/liquids with low melting points, and whether they conduct electricity: (a) CH₄, (b) SiO₂, (c) C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose). Explain using bonding type.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify bonding type for each compound.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) CH₄: covalent molecular; (b) SiO₂: covalent network; (c) C₆H₁₂O₆: covalent molecular

step_by_step.explanation: Predict melting points based on bonding.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) CH₄: weak London forces → low mp (-182°C), gas; (b) SiO₂: network covalent → very high mp (1710°C), solid; (c) C₆H₁₂O₆: H-bonding → moderate mp (146°C), solid

step_by_step.explanation: Predict electrical conductivity.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) CH₄: no ions/electrons → no conductivity; (b) SiO₂: no free charges → no conductivity; (c) C₆H₁₂O₆: no ions in solution → no conductivity

step_by_step.explanation: Verify predictions with known values.
step_by_step.calculation: All predictions match experimental values ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CH₄: covalent molecular → gas at room temp, mp -182°C, no conductivity. (b) SiO₂: covalent network → solid, mp 1710°C, no conductivity. (c) C₆H₁₂O₆: covalent molecular with H-bonding → solid, mp 146°C, no conductivity in solution. Bonding type (molecular vs. network) determines physical properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting physical properties from covalent bonding type (molecular vs. network)

sections.section_title: Writing Formulas and Names for Covalent Compounds
sections.introduction: Covalent compounds follow systematic naming conventions using prefixes to indicate the number of each atom in the molecule. Unlike ionic compounds, covalent compound names specify exact molecular composition.

key_concept.definition: Covalent compound names use Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, etc.) to indicate the number of each atom; the second element ends in -ide; first element prefix 'mono-' is usually omitted.
key_concept.context: This naming system (IUPAC) precisely specifies molecular composition; essential because covalent compounds can have multiple stoichiometries (CO vs. CO₂).

classifications.value: Compounds containing two different nonmetal elements
characteristics: Vowel dropped if prefix ends in 'a' or 'o' before 'oxide'
classifications.behavior: Name = (prefix)(first element) + (prefix)(second element root)-ide; prefixes: mono(1), di(2), tri(3), tetra(4), penta(5), hexa(6), etc.

examples.explanation: C = carbon (mono- omitted); O = oxide. Name: carbon monoxide (not carbon monooxide; 'o' dropped). Formula shows 1:1 ratio.

image.alt: Carbon monoxide naming convention
image.caption: Binary covalent naming: carbon monoxide (mono- omitted for first element).

examples.explanation: C = carbon (mono- omitted); O₂ = dioxide. Name: carbon dioxide. Formula shows 1:2 ratio. Different compound from CO with different properties.

image.alt: Carbon dioxide naming convention
image.caption: Binary covalent naming: carbon dioxide (di- prefix shows 2 oxygen atoms).

examples.explanation: N₂ = dinitrogen; O₄ = tetroxide (tetra- → tetr- before oxide). Name: dinitrogen tetroxide. Both prefixes included (not first element mono-).

image.alt: Dinitrogen tetroxide naming convention
image.caption: Binary covalent naming: both prefixes used when more than one of each atom.

examples.explanation: P = phosphorus (mono- omitted); Cl₅ = pentachloride. Name: phosphorus pentachloride. Expanded octet on P (10 electrons).

image.alt: Phosphorus pentachloride naming convention
image.caption: Binary covalent naming: phosphorus pentachloride (penta- shows 5 chlorine atoms).

classifications.value: Some covalent compounds have well-established common names used instead of systematic names
classifications.behavior: Common names persist for important compounds; systematic names still used in formal chemistry contexts.

examples.explanation: Systematic: dihydrogen monoxide (rarely used). Common: water (universally used). H₂O is essential for life, common name preferred.

image.alt: Water common name vs. systematic name
image.caption: Common name: water is used instead of systematic dihydrogen monoxide.

examples.explanation: Systematic: nitrogen trihydride (rarely used). Common: ammonia (universally used). Important industrial chemical and cleaning agent.

image.alt: Ammonia common name vs. systematic name
image.caption: Common name: ammonia is used instead of systematic nitrogen trihydride.

examples.explanation: Systematic: carbon tetrahydride (rarely used). Common: methane (universally used). Simplest hydrocarbon, main component of natural gas.

image.alt: Methane common name vs. systematic name
image.caption: Common name: methane is used instead of systematic carbon tetrahydride.

principles.statement: Greek prefixes indicate atom count: mono(1), di(2), tri(3), tetra(4), penta(5), hexa(6), hepta(7), octa(8), nona(9), deca(10).
principles.application: Enables naming any binary covalent compound from formula; enables writing formula from name.
principles.example: N₂O₃: di- = 2 N, tri- = 3 O → dinitrogen trioxide. Sulfur hexafluoride: hexa- = 6 F → SF₆.
principles.analogy: Like apartment numbers: prefix tells you how many 'rooms' (atoms) of each type in the molecule.

image.alt: Table of Greek prefixes for covalent compound naming
image.caption: Covalent prefixes: Greek prefixes indicate number of each atom in the molecule.

principles.statement: When prefix ends in 'a' or 'o' and element name begins with vowel (usually 'o' for oxide), drop the final vowel of the prefix.
principles.application: Ensures smooth pronunciation: tetraoxide → tetroxide; monooxide → monoxide; pentaoxide → pentoxide.
principles.example: CO = carbon monoxide (not monooxide); N₂O₄ = dinitrogen tetroxide (not tetraoxide); Cl₂O₇ = dichlorine heptoxide (not heptaoxide).
principles.analogy: Like contractions in speech: 'do not' → 'don't' for easier pronunciation.

worked_examples.title: Naming Covalent Compounds from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following covalent compounds: (a) PCl₃, (b) SO₃, (c) N₂O, (d) Cl₂O₇.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify number of each atom from subscripts.
Apply prefixes (omit mono- for first element).
step_by_step.calculation: (a) P = phosphorus, Cl₃ = trichloride; (b) S = sulfur, O₃ = trioxide; (c) N₂ = dinitrogen, O = monoxide; (d) Cl₂ = dichlorine, O₇ = heptoxide

step_by_step.explanation: Apply vowel dropping rule where needed.
step_by_step.calculation: (c) monooxide → monoxide; (d) heptaoxide → heptoxide

step_by_step.explanation: Combine names.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) phosphorus trichloride; (b) sulfur trioxide; (c) dinitrogen monoxide; (d) dichlorine heptoxide

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) PCl₃: phosphorus trichloride. (b) SO₃: sulfur trioxide. (c) N₂O: dinitrogen monoxide (also called nitrous oxide). (d) Cl₂O₇: dichlorine heptoxide. Prefixes indicate exact atom counts; 'mono-' omitted for first element; vowels dropped before 'oxide'.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming binary covalent compounds using Greek prefixes

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas from Covalent Compound Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) sulfur hexafluoride, (b) dinitrogen pentoxide, (c) carbon tetrachloride, (d) phosphorus tribromide.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify elements and prefixes from names.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) S + hexa(6) F; (b) di(2) N + pent(5) O; (c) C + tetra(4) Cl; (d) P + tri(3) Br

step_by_step.explanation: Convert prefixes to subscripts.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) SF₆; (b) N₂O₅; (c) CCl₄; (d) PBr₃

step_by_step.explanation: Verify no 'mono-' prefix means subscript 1.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) S has no prefix → S₁ = S; (c) C has no prefix → C₁ = C; (d) P has no prefix → P₁ = P

step_by_step.explanation: Write final formulas.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) SF₆; (b) N₂O₅; (c) CCl₄; (d) PBr₃

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Sulfur hexafluoride: SF₆. (b) Dinitrogen pentoxide: N₂O₅. (c) Carbon tetrachloride: CCl₄. (d) Phosphorus tribromide: PBr₃. Prefixes directly convert to subscripts; 'mono-' (1) is omitted in names but implied in formulas.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing covalent compound formulas from systematic names

summary: Covalent bonding occurs through electron sharing between atoms (typically nonmetals) to achieve stable noble gas configurations. Atoms share electron pairs: single bond (1 pair, 2 e⁻), double bond (2 pairs, 4 e⁻), triple bond (3 pairs, 6 e⁻). Bond strength increases and length decreases with multiplicity: triple > double > single. Covalent bonds form through orbital overlap, with shared electrons attracted to both nuclei. Lewis structures represent covalent bonding: count valence electrons, arrange atoms, distribute electrons to satisfy octets (8 e⁻ for most atoms, 2 e⁻ for H), form multiple bonds if needed. Exceptions include incomplete octets (Be: 4 e⁻, B: 6 e⁻), expanded octets (Period 3+ elements like S, P, Cl can have >8 e⁻ using d orbitals), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals like NO, NO₂). Covalent compounds have lower melting/boiling points than ionic (weak intermolecular forces between molecules), poor electrical conductivity (no free ions/electrons), and solubility depends on polarity (polar dissolves in polar, nonpolar in nonpolar). Network covalent compounds (diamond, SiO₂) are exceptions with very high melting points. Binary covalent names use Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, penta-, hexa-) to indicate atom counts; second element ends in -ide; first element 'mono-' omitted; vowels dropped before 'oxide' (monooxide → monoxide). Common names exist for important compounds (H₂O = water, NH₃ = ammonia, CH₄ = methane). Understanding covalent bonding enables prediction of molecular structure, properties, and reactivity for the vast majority of chemical compounds.
